page: Amazon Web Services m1.medium is a first-generation general-purpose server instance. It features a single dedicated physical core and vCPU powered by an Intel Xeon E5-2651 v2 processor running at 1.8 GHz on the i386 architecture. The instance is provisioned with 4.0 GB of system memory, translating to 3.7 GB per core, and is managed via the Xen hypervisor. It includes 410 GB of local HDD storage, but lacks any GPU acceleration or baseline network bandwidth.

Benchmark results place the m1.medium in the lower tiers of modern cloud performance. The stress-ng single-core and multi-core CPU tests both scored 1284.79, reflecting its single-threaded limitation. Memory bandwidth is constrained, with read-only large block transfers reaching 7.84 GB/sec and write operations at 6.77 GB/sec. Geekbench scores further highlight these limitations, with a single-core score of 489.0 and a multi-core score of 491.0. Cryptographic performance is also low, with a Passmark CPU encryption score of 167.39, despite the presence of hardware AES support.

Given its legacy hardware profile and low benchmark scores across database operations (279.36 on Passmark) and Redis SET operations (0.45 million operations per second), the m1.medium is not suited for modern production workloads. However, its inclusion of 410 GB of local HDD storage provides a significant amount of legacy storage capacity relative to its compute resources. This makes it a qualitatively cost-efficient option for legacy application hosting, basic file storage, or low-traffic static web serving, which achieved a throughput of 1.13 GB/sec in testing.
description: Amazon Web Services m1.medium is a legacy, first-generation general-purpose instance featuring a single dedicated Intel Xeon E5-2651 v2 core running at 1.8 GHz on the i386 architecture. It is equipped with 4.0 GB of RAM, 410 GB of local HDD storage, and operates under the Xen hypervisor. Performance benchmarks place this instance in the lower percentiles, with a Geekbench single-core score of 489.0 and a multi-core score of 491.0. Memory bandwidth is limited, reaching 7.84 GB/sec for large block reads. Lacking modern instruction sets like AVX and baseline network bandwidth, the m1.medium is best suited for legacy applications, basic file storage, or low-traffic static web serving where high compute performance is not required.
og_description: Amazon Web Services m1.medium is a legacy general-purpose instance featuring 1 vCPU on an Intel Xeon E5-2651 v2 processor, 4.0 GB of RAM, and 410 GB of local HDD storage under the Xen hypervisor.
meta_description: Amazon Web Services m1.medium is a legacy general-purpose instance with 1 vCPU, 4.0 GB of RAM, and 410 GB of local HDD storage.
tagline: A legacy general-purpose instance featuring a single dedicated core, 4 GB of memory, and substantial local HDD storage for basic workloads.
bullet_points: Single dedicated Intel Xeon E5-2651 v2 core at 1.8 GHz
4.0 GB of system memory and 410 GB of local HDD storage
Legacy i386 architecture running on the Xen hypervisor
Low-tier CPU and memory bandwidth performance in benchmarks
Best suited for legacy applications and low-traffic static web serving
